section_title: INTRODUCTION TO THE MARKET MAKER SERIES REVAMP
content:
Welcome to the revamped 2014 video series, 'Market Maker Series'. The original series had 12 long
- winded videos. With the support studies available on this YouTube channel, the aim is to polish and deliver
a more meaningful and impactful series for your trading.
It's important to note that many market maker buzzwords circulating on social media today originated from
this series or my 1996 lecture nights. Many have taken pieces of my teachings over the last three decades
and offered their own interpretations, which have often been found wanting. My goal here is to present this
information concisely and succinctly, without oversimplification.
Instead of 12 videos, this revamped series will be delivered in five volumes, putting everything in proper
context and order. This first volume is dedicated to 'Understanding the Macro'.

So, who is the market maker? The central banks are the producers of currency prices and are the true
market makers. They establish and manage a country's currency price at all times, both autonomously and
manually. Currency prices are 100 percent controlled and manipulated by central banks.
Investment bank dealers (like Goldman Sachs, UBS, City) or brokers are not market makers, despite often
calling themselves such. In the currency (Forex) markets, the central bank is the market maker. Everybody
else deals, trades, and exchanges on their product, which is the price of the commodity or currency. Buyers
and sellers do not make the price; we only exchange at the price feed given to us. Dealers and brokers do
business with the price feeds set and delivered by central banks; they do not set or control currency prices.

section_title: CENTRAL BANK ALGORITHMS AND RETAIL LOGIC
content:
Central banks are not following retail indicators or flawed retail logic. Their algorithms do not consider
Wyckoff, Harmonic, Elliott Wave, VSA (Volume Spread Analysis), supply and demand zones, or any other
retail concepts. None of these are considered when central bank algorithms deliver price, reprice, or hold
price in consolidation. This is simply a fact.
Central banks employ high-tech algorithms to deliver currency price feeds, and they do not run on supply
and demand. This point is crucial: supply and demand has no bearing on the delivery of currency prices,
period.

To understand real market maker concepts, you must start with quantitative analysis. Quantitative analysis
is statistical and measurable; it is not subjective or left for interpretation, unlike asking 20 traders if a trend
is bullish or bearish, which yields different interpretations. This is qualitative, and we will get into that later.
For now, we begin with quantitative analysis, which is measurable, statistical, and straightforward.
When performing quantitative analysis, you're asking: 'What should the market be experiencing at the
present moment?' You need a way to determine if the market is bullish or bearish, and where to derive that
answer.

The primary sources for this information are:
- Global Interest Rates: Available at www.global-rates.com. You should bookmark the specific page for
central bank interest rates. This is the fundamental driver that sets the tone for long-term macro price
movement.
- Commitment of Traders (COT): Available at barchart.com. The COT report shows a visual representation
of what smart money is doing and how they are positioned periodically.
These are suggested, free websites. You don't need subscriptions or specific platforms. By blending these
two elements, you gain a real, measurable, and quantitative perspective on what smart money (market
makers) is interpreting for price on a long-term basis. This is how you start to answer whether a market is
bullish or bearish, without relying on trend lines or moving averages. This is what interbank traders concern
themselves with.

By looking at these two specific points of interest (Global Interest Rates and COT), you will come up with
either a 'Buy Program' or a 'Sell Program'.
To access COT data on barchart.com: Assume we are pulling up a currency like the Japanese Yen. Click
on the 'Select Commodity' tab, then choose 'Currencies' from the dropdown, and select the desired
currency (e.g., Japanese Yen). On the resulting page, you're looking for the futures contract with the
highest open interest, not the 'cash' line. For example, if September 2021 has the highest open interest,
select that. Open interest shows the available open interest in that particular contract month. After a
contract expires (e.g., September), it rolls over to the next (e.g., December), and its open interest will rise.

Once you select the appropriate futures contract on barchart.com, you will be taken to a page where you
need to click on the 'Interactive Chart' link in the upper left-hand corner. On the interactive chart, locate the
'+ Study' tab and click it. When prompted to add an indicator, type 'Commitment of Traders'. As you type
'com', it should appear as an option. Click it.
A small window will open. Click the little 'eyeball' icon next to 'Small Specs' to put a line through it, hiding it.
Then click the 'Add' button. This will apply the Commitment of Traders graph to your chart, typically at the
bottom. While you can toggle it to the top, it's historically seen underneath price charts.

section_title: ACCESSING AND SORTING GLOBAL INTEREST RATES
content:
For global interest rates, go to global-rates.com. Navigate to 'Interest Rates' and then 'Central Bank's
Breakdown or Summary of Current Interest Rates'. This page will display a table of interest rates. Each
column is active and can sort like a spreadsheet. You can sort by 'Country or Region' alphabetically, by
'Current Rate' from high to low or low to high, or by 'Change in Direction'. You can also search for previous
rates and last change dates.
For now, focus on sorting the 'Current Rate' column from high to low or low to high to easily find rates.
You're primarily interested in the major currencies: Swiss Franc, Japanese Yen, European interest rate
(ECB), British Pound, United States Fed rate, Canada's bank rate, New Zealand's bank rate, and
Australia's bank rate.

An interest rate differential is simply the difference between one country's interest rate and another's. This
is important because, at the central bank and interbank levels, large capital flows seek yield, meaning they
are always looking for a higher rate of return. For example, you would prefer to invest in a country offering a
five percent interest rate over one offering two percent. Large flows, driven by interest, yield, and
speculation, operate under the same logic.
To apply this in Forex, you need to find a high-yielding interest rate country's currency and couple it with a
weak currency. For instance, consider Japan with a negative 0.10 interest rate. Comparing this to the US
Fed's current rate of 0.25 percent, the US Dollar has the higher interest rate. The differential is positive for
the Dollar and negative for the Yen.

section_title: COMBINING COT AND INTEREST RATE DIFFERENTIAL (USD/JPY EXAMPLE)
content:
With the fundamental backdrop of a higher US interest rate relative to Japan, we then look at the
Commitment of Traders chart. While I typically don't prefer plotting COT on the Dollar Index (DXY) due to
potentially limited information, we'll examine it here. We're interested in the red line, representing
'Commercials' (which we can equate to central banks). Note that being above or below the zero-basis line
doesn't singularly mean buy or sell.
What we seek is a combination of the interest rate differential and the highest net long position (or lowest
net short position) held by the commercials. Given the Dollar's relatively higher interest rate compared to
the Yen, we look for a time when DXY commercials are net long (above the zero line). In late 2020 to
January 2021, the red line on the DXY chart showed commercials were net long at a level not seen in
years, indicating a bullish bias.
This DXY bullishness is only confirmed if we see the opposite with the Japanese Yen. As we transition from
December into January 2021, the Yen's COT chart shows commercials were never this net short,
suggesting they believe the Yen is overbought and likely to decline. Anytime in the past on this chart, they
haven't been as bearish (below the zero line) as they were here. By itself, this doesn't mean anything; you
need to see a 'break in market structure'.

section_title: MARKET STRUCTURE BREAK AND ALGORITHM REPRICING
content:
Looking at the Japanese Yen chart, identify a 'swing low' a low with a higher low to its left and right. For
me, a 'market structure break' occurs when a previous swing high (the highest point before the breakdown)
is broken. Once this occurs, all the conditions align: bearish for Yen, bullish for Dollar, and the interest rate
differential kicks in.
At this point, the central bank algorithms will start to reprice the Yen lower and the Dollar higher, or
consolidate the Dollar. We've recently seen the Dollar consolidate while the Yen was allowed to reprice
lower. This translates directly to a Forex chart like USD/JPY. Pairing the stronger (Dollar) against the
weaker (Yen) based on these differentials means the USD/JPY pair should be bullish, expecting the Dollar
to rise and the Yen to drop. This results in the USD/JPY rallying higher, as observed from December into
January.

section_title: REAL MARKET MOVEMENTS AND TRADING STYLES
content:
The observed rally in USD/JPY from December into January is significant. This approach forms the basis
for understanding how to find markets poised for movement, using an interbank trader's perspective, not
subjective retail indicators. It's about real, measurable, and quantitative means of determining market
direction, providing a solid foundation for most trades.
Trading in this direction (e.g., buying USD/JPY when bullish) allows for various trading styles: short-term
trading, day trading, scalping, position trading, or swing trading. Every discipline can benefit from aligning
with this macro bias. You can be a short-term buyer, day trader, scalper, or swing trader during the upward
move. This directional bias guides your trade selection.

Let's examine a case study: CAD/JPY. Again, we have the Japanese Yen with a weak interest rate
(negative 0.10). The Canadian Dollar's interest rate is 0.25 percent, the same as the US Dollar. Therefore,
we should anticipate a bullish Canadian Dollar and a bearish Japanese Yen. However, this is just the
precursor; we must perform a quantitative analysis of smart money's actual positions via the Commitment
of Traders report.
The interest rate differential provides the fundamental underpinning, but it doesn't mean the chart is always
a buy or sell. You need to wait for smart money to position themselves, and then the algorithm will reprice.

section_title: CAD FUTURES COT ANALYSIS AND RATE CHANGES
content:
Looking at the Canadian Dollar futures market (September 2021 contract on barchart.com), in March 2020,
the Canadian interest rate dropped by half a percent (from 0.75 percent to 0.25 percent). Even with this
drop, 0.25 percent is still better than Japan's negative 0.10 percent, maintaining a positive differential.
Around the time of the interest rate announcement and the price's deep dive in March 2020, we see a
significant buildup of net long positions by the commercials on the COT chart. They are above the zero line,
indicating a bullish stance. This bullish positioning occurs when the interest rate differential is positive for
the Canadian Dollar and negative for the Yen. Later in 2020, even as the commercials move below the zero
line, it doesn't negate the bullish bias; it merely indicates a strong trend where they are hedging. Relative
equal highs are seen as buy stops to be taken out. From a long-term perspective, these highs are
challenged and often taken out.

While the Canadian interest rate dropped, it remained positive relative to Japan's unchanged negative rate
since 2016. So, we have the real positioning of smart money being bullish after the interest rate
announcement. Even with the dip below the zero line, it signifies a strong trend, and commercials are
simply hedging. As price drops, they buy back their heavy net short positions (from earlier highs) to exit.
This is all we're seeing.
This behavior is also linked to seasonal tendencies. For instance, the Canadian Dollar has specific
seasonal tendencies around certain times of the year, which align with these market movements, further
validating the analysis.

section_title: CAD/JPY FOREX CHART AND CENTRAL BANK MOTIVES
content:
Revisiting the Japanese Yen COT chart, we see commercials remain bearish. A crossover on the COT
doesn't instantly change a bearish bias to bullish; it simply shows a strong trend where commercials are
buying back their shorts as price drops. This translates to the CAD/JPY Forex chart. In November
- December, following a market structure break, the CAD/JPY pair rallies, reflecting the underlying bullish
Canadian Dollar and bearish Japanese Yen. This results in a significant price run of over 1200 points.
These large moves are what interbank traders, even at the central bank level, are looking for. Despite
arguments that central banks don't trade, they employ people to trade for them, speculate, and seek profit
through every avenue. They control price and the entire market. There's never been an audit on a central
bank; they operate without external policing. They have the inside edge and advantage. Rather than
viewing this as unfair, embracing 'this is how they do it' allows you to operate with that understanding.

section_title: THE POWER OF INFORMATION AND TRADING PERSONALITY
content:
This information is extremely powerful, providing insight that puts you on the right side of the marketplace
for long-term biases, not just single-day moves. While I personally don't hold positions from a low in
November all the way to a high in June due to my short-term trading personality and preference for surgical
strikes, this doesn't mean you shouldn't.
Matching your trading personality to your mode of trading is crucial. If you're committed to an idea and don't
easily change your mind, you might be an excellent long-term position trader. If you have less patience, you
might be a swing trader (holding for a few days to a week). For even less patience, you might be a day
trader or scalper.
For day traders and scalpers, during a period identified as bullish (like the CAD/JPY rally), every bullish day
is an opportunity for intraday price moves. Down days might be losses or times to wait for new setups.
Scalpers might use higher leverage on bullish trades and less when attempting to short. It's generally
advised not to short a fundamentally driven upward move, as everything is predisposed to go higher.
Hopefully, this first installment clarifies the mystery behind market movements. We will dive deeper into
what makes these markets gyrate in Volume 2.
